flashcards.Back: An open-source automation tool that is agentless and uses a push model. it communicates with network devices via SSH or NETCONF and uses YAML for configuration.
A configuration management tool that typically uses an agent-based pull model. it uses its own domain-specific language to define the desired state of a device.
An automation platform that uses an agent-based pull model. it relies on Ruby-based code to define 'recipes' for managing device configurations.
A management model (used by Ansible) where no specialized software needs to be installed on the network devices themselves to perform automation.
A management model (used by Puppet and Chef) where a software client must be installed on each managed device to communicate with the central server.
An automation workflow where the central management server initiates the connection and sends configuration changes to the network devices immediately.
An automation workflow where the managed device periodically contacts the central server to check for and download any pending configuration updates.
A YAML file that contains a series of 'plays' or instructions to be executed on a group of network devices to achieve a desired configuration.
A file that uses Puppet's declarative language to describe the final desired state of a system's configuration.
A file containing Ruby-based code that defines the specific steps and logic needed to configure a resource on a managed device.
A file used by Ansible to list and group the IP addresses or hostnames of all the network devices that it will manage.
A container used by Chef to organize and group multiple related recipes, templates, and other configuration files into a single unit.
An online repository where Puppet users share pre-built modules and manifests for managing common network devices and applications.
Yet Another Markup Language. A human-friendly data serialization language used by Ansible for playbooks and inventory files.
The programming language that Chef is built upon and uses for its configuration scripts (recipes and cookbooks).
The property where running an automation task multiple times results in the same final configuration, ensuring no changes are made if the device is already in the desired state.
The tendency for device configurations to change over time due to manual CLI edits. automation tools detect and correct this drift automatically.
The computer or server where Ansible is installed and from which all playbooks and management commands are executed.
The centralized server in a Puppet environment that stores all configuration manifests and manages the communication with the agents.
The central hub where all Chef cookbooks are stored and from which managed nodes pull their configuration recipes.
A powerful templating engine used by Ansible to generate dynamic network configuration files based on variables.
A management style where the engineer defines 'what' the end state should look like (e.g., VLAN 10 exists), rather than 'how' to do it.
A management style where the engineer provides the specific commands or steps to achieve a change (similar to a standard script).
A specialized piece of code that Ansible uses to perform a specific task on a device, such as the 'ios_config' or 'ios_command' modules.
An ordered list of recipes that defines the sequence in which Chef will apply configuration changes to a managed node.
A specialized Puppet agent that runs on a separate server to manage devices (like some Cisco switches) that cannot host an internal agent.
The use of tools like Git to track history, manage branches, and collaborate on the configuration files used for network automation.
A quick, one-line Ansible command used to perform a simple task on many devices at once without writing a full playbook.
The fundamental unit of configuration, such as a single file, a package, or a network interface, that is managed by the tool.
The configuration configuration defined in the automation tool's master files. the tool's goal is to ensure the device's actual state matches this.
